blocks.content: Nathan saw Thomas Hailey coming from a comfortable distance away.
Which was enough distance to notice the expression — the measured, unhurried expression of a man who has decided to have a conversation and is not concerned about how it lands.
Nathan stood.
Maya looked at him, then at her father, then back at Nathan, and said nothing, which was itself a statement.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: Nathan.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: Mr. Hailey.
A pause.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: I — we were just — it was a conversation, sir. I wasn't—
Thomas Hailey held up a hand.
Nathan stopped.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: I'm going to tell you something.
Nathan prepared himself.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: My daughter is twenty-four years old and she has spent three years making herself emotionally unavailable to every person who has tried to get close to her. Not because she doesn't feel things. Because she feels them very strongly and has decided the safest policy is to keep them at a managed distance.
Nathan listened. The fire moved.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: Fifteen minutes ago I watched her laugh without calculating it first.
A long beat.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: She gets that from her mother. That sweetness underneath all the armor. Her mother was exactly the same. You had to be patient enough to wait it out.
His voice was level. Not threatening, not sentimental. Just true.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: I have been waiting three years for someone to make that laugh happen again. I didn't expect it to be tonight, and I didn't expect it to be you. But it was.
He extended his hand.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Hailey
blocks.content: Don't take it lightly.
Nathan shook it. Firmly, without hesitation.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: I won't.

Thomas Hailey nodded once — the same nod he had given Ethan in a different conversation not long ago. The nod of a man who has said what needed to be said and trusts the person standing in front of him to carry it.
He picked up his glass. Walked back toward the deck.

Nathan stood at the edge of the fire pit and looked at Maya, who had watched the entire exchange with the expression of someone who had lost control of a situation and was still deciding how she felt about that.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I apologize for my father.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: I like your father.
He sat back down on the driftwood beside her.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: You're impossible.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: I've been told.
The flush in her cheeks hadn't fully faded. He did not point this out.
